Task id: T82 (ledgerRefs goals:G4)
Base commit: a4ad01d (main)
Branch: implement/T82 — your worktree is created by native isolation; FIRST run `git switch -c implement/T82` inside YOUR worktree so your commit lands on the task branch the orchestrator merges.

NOTE: this is a Go repository (wanbond). The check gate is `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c` if go is not on PATH (per AGENTS.md); there is no bun here. A sibling task (T81) is concurrently adding validate() rules for per-peer psk/name — do NOT implement validation; keep this task strictly to the helper so the merge stays clean.

headline: Add config helper resolving each peer's effective PSK and identity

description: Add a config method/function returning, per configured peer, its effective PSK (top-level Config.PSK for the single-peer back-compat case; the peer's own psk when >1 peer) plus its stable name/id. This is the single source device.Up and the Bind consume so back-compat lives in exactly one place.

acceptance: Unit test: for a single-peer config the helper returns the top-level psk; for a multi-peer config it returns each peer's own psk and its name/id; ordering is stable and matches cfg.WireGuard.Peers. `go test ./internal/config/...` passes.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	package config
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/base64"
5		"errors"
6		"fmt"
7		"math"
8		"net"
9		"net/netip"
10		"strconv"
11		"strings"
12		"time"
13	
14		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
15	)
16	
17	// Role selects which end of the tunnel this process runs as. It is an explicit,
18	// required field; the role is never inferred from other configuration.
19	type Role string
20	
21	const (
22		// RoleEdge is the mobile Linux box that bonds the WAN uplinks.
23		RoleEdge Role = "edge"
24		// RoleConcentrator is the public-IP VPS that terminates the tunnel.
25		RoleConcentrator Role = "concentrator"
26	)
27	
28	func (r Role) valid() bool {
29		return r == RoleEdge || r == RoleConcentrator
30	}
31	
32	// keyLen is the byte length of a Curve25519 WireGuard key and of a raw
33	// outer-control PSK, before base64 encoding.
34	const keyLen = 32
35	
36	// Config is the whole wanbond configuration, shared by both roles and parsed
37	// from a single TOML file.
38	type Config struct {
39		Role      Role            `toml:"role"`
40		Paths     []Path          `toml:"paths"`
41		WireGuard WireGuard       `toml:"wireguard"`
42		Amnezia   Amnezia         `toml:"amnezia"`
43		PSK       Key             `toml:"psk"`
44		Metrics   Metrics         `toml:"metrics"`
45		Log       Log             `toml:"log"`
46		Scheduler SchedulerConfig `toml:"scheduler"`
47		FEC       FEC             `toml:"fec"`
48	}
49	
50	// SchedulerPolicy selects the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind
51	// runs. It is a bounded enum: the P1 active-backup failover (default) or the T21
52	// weighted-aggregation policy. The policy is an explicit config choice — active-
53	// backup stays selectable so the P1 behaviour is never removed, only extended.
54	type SchedulerPolicy string
55	
56	const (
57		// PolicyActiveBackup is the P1 single-active-path failover scheduler: all egress
58		// on the highest-priority live path, every other path idle (data-thrift by
59		// construction). It is the default when [scheduler] is omitted.
60		PolicyActiveBackup SchedulerPolicy = "active-backup"
61		// PolicyWeighted is the T21 weighted-aggregation scheduler: under load a single
62		// flow is striped across both paths in proportion to per-path RTT/loss-derived
63		// weight, collapsing to the primary at low load (5G stays ~idle) with hysteresis,
64		// and per-path send-pacing to bound bufferbloat.
65		PolicyWeighted SchedulerPolicy = "weighted"
66	)
67	
68	func (p SchedulerPolicy) valid() bool {
69		return p == PolicyActiveBackup || p == PolicyWeighted
70	}
71	
72	// Weighted-aggregation policy defaults (T21). They are applied when [scheduler]
73	// selects the weighted policy but leaves a knob at its zero value, so a minimal
74	// `policy = "weighted"` block is usable without hand-tuning every threshold. They
75	// are conservative bring-up values; the empirical per-path pace is sized from a
76	// measured BDP in a bandwidth-capped fixture (P0 findings §7, deferred to T35/T23),
77	// so the shipped default leaves pacing DISABLED and only bounds distribution.
78	const (
79		// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity, in frame-selection
80		// slots per second, that the aggregation load-gate compares offered load against
81		// AND (when pacing is enabled) the per-path token-bucket refill rate. It is a
82		// synthetic proxy for path capacity: there is no measured BDP (P0 §7), so capacity
83		// is expressed in the only unit the scheduler sees — Pick() invocations per second.
84		defaultPerPathCapacityFPS = 10000.0
85		// defaultEngageFraction engages aggregation once offered load exceeds this
86		// fraction of one path's capacity — i.e. a single path is nearly saturated.
87		defaultEngageFraction = 0.9
88		// defaultDisengageFraction collapses back to primary-only once offered load falls
89		// below this fraction of one path's capacity. It is strictly below the engage
90		// fraction: the gap is the hysteresis band that stops the 5G path from dribbling
91		// on/off around a single threshold (requirement 2, data-thrift).
92		defaultDisengageFraction = 0.5
93		// defaultCollapseDwell is how long offered load must stay continuously below the
94		// disengage fraction before aggregation collapses to primary-only — the temporal
95		// half of the hysteresis, mirroring the active-backup failback dwell so a brief
96		// lull does not repeatedly park then re-engage the metered path.
97		defaultCollapseDwell = 2 * time.Second
98		// defaultLoadTau is the time constant of the exponentially-weighted offered-load
99		// rate estimator. It smooths the instantaneous Pick() arrival rate so the gate
100		// reacts to sustained load, not single-frame bursts.
101		defaultLoadTau = 200 * time.Millisecond
102		// defaultPacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst (in frame slots)
103		// when pacing is enabled: the bucket admits up to this many frames instantaneously
104		// before the refill rate binds, absorbing normal jitter without inflating a queue.
105		defaultPacingBurstFrames = 64.0
106		// defaultWeightRTTFloor floors the RTT in the weight formula so a path reporting a
107		// near-zero RTT (cold estimator, no samples yet) cannot be handed unbounded weight.
108		defaultWeightRTTFloor = 1 * time.Millisecond
109		// defaultWeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root so a zero-loss
110		// path gets a large-but-finite weight and two zero-loss paths split by inverse-RTT
111		// alone rather than both diverging.
112		defaultWeightLossFloor = 1e-3
113	)

115	// SchedulerConfig selects and tunes the send scheduler. When the [scheduler] block
116	// is omitted the policy defaults to active-backup and every weighted knob is
117	// ignored, so existing configs keep the P1 behaviour unchanged. The weighted knobs
118	// are validated (and defaulted) only when the weighted policy is selected.
119	type SchedulerConfig struct {
120		// Policy selects the scheduler; defaults to active-backup when empty.
121		Policy SchedulerPolicy `toml:"policy"`
122	
123		// PerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity in frame-selection slots
124		// per second: the denominator the aggregation load-gate compares offered load
125		// against, and the per-path pacing refill rate when PacingEnabled. Must be > 0
126		// under the weighted policy.
127		PerPathCapacityFPS float64 `toml:"per_path_capacity_fps"`
128		// EngageFraction engages aggregation when offered load exceeds
129		// EngageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS. Must be in (0,1].
130		EngageFraction float64 `toml:"engage_fraction"`
131		// DisengageFraction collapses aggregation to primary-only once offered load stays
132		// below DisengageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS for CollapseDwell. Must be in
133		// [0,EngageFraction) — strictly below EngageFraction so the two form a hysteresis
134		// band.
135		DisengageFraction float64 `toml:"disengage_fraction"`
136		// CollapseDwell is the sustained-low-load dwell before collapsing to primary-only
137		// (hysteresis). Must be >= 0.
138		CollapseDwell time.Duration `toml:"collapse_dwell"`
139		// LoadTau is the offered-load rate estimator's time constant. Must be > 0.
140		LoadTau time.Duration `toml:"load_tau"`
141	
142		// PacingEnabled turns per-path send-pacing on. When false the token buckets are
143		// bypassed (a documented no-op — P0 §7 could not empirically size the pace in the
144		// unmetered fixture), but PerPathCapacityFPS still drives the aggregation gate.
145		PacingEnabled bool `toml:"pacing_enabled"`
146		// PacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst in frame slots. Must be > 0
147		// when PacingEnabled.
148		PacingBurstFrames float64 `toml:"pacing_burst_frames"`
149	
150		// WeightRTTFloor floors RTT in the weight formula (must be > 0 under weighted).
151		WeightRTTFloor time.Duration `toml:"weight_rtt_floor"`
152		// WeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root (must be > 0 under
153		// weighted).
154		WeightLossFloor float64 `toml:"weight_loss_floor"`
155	}

157	// BDPSizing is a per-path pacing sizing derived from a MEASURED path, in the
158	// frame-selection-slot units the weighted scheduler pace uses (defect D22). It is the
159	// empirical alternative to the synthetic frame-count defaults: CapacityFPS replaces
160	// PerPathCapacityFPS and BurstFrames replaces PacingBurstFrames.
161	type BDPSizing struct {
162		// CapacityFPS is the sustained per-path frame rate the measured bottleneck bandwidth
163		// supports (the token-bucket refill rate and aggregation-gate denominator).
164		CapacityFPS float64
165		// BurstFrames is the bandwidth-delay product expressed in frames (bandwidth x RTT,
166		// i.e. one RTT of in-flight frames): the token-bucket burst that absorbs one RTT of
167		// jitter without building a standing queue.
168		BurstFrames float64
169	}
170	
171	// defaultAvgWireFrameBytes is the conservative average on-wire outer-frame size used
172	// when deriving the per-path pace from an operator-declared bandwidth at config load
173	// (T53): a full IPv4 path MTU, since a full-MTU DATA datagram occupies about one path
174	// MTU on the wire. It mirrors bind.DefaultPathMTU (1500) rather than importing it —
175	// internal/bind imports internal/config, so config cannot import bind without a cycle
176	// (the same mirror-with-cross-reference pattern as maxFECDeadline above). Sizing
177	// capacity with the full-MTU frame is the conservative floor: smaller average frames
178	// would yield a HIGHER frame rate, so this never over-paces a path.
179	const defaultAvgWireFrameBytes = 1500.0
180	
181	// SizePacingFromBDP derives the weighted scheduler's per-path pacing parameters from a
182	// measured path instead of the synthetic frame-count default (defect D22). The shipped
183	// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS (10000, ~115 Mbit/s at full MTU) sits far above a realistic
184	// slow uplink, so the aggregation gate may never engage and — with pacing enabled — the
185	// pace never binds; deriving capacity from the measured bottleneck bandwidth fixes both.
186	//
187	// capacity_fps = bandwidth / (8 * avg wire frame bytes) — frames/s the link sustains.
188	// burst_frames = capacity_fps * RTT — one RTT of in-flight frames (equivalently
189	// BDP_bytes / avg wire frame bytes, since the bandwidth-delay product BDP = bandwidth * RTT).
190	//
191	// bandwidthBitsPerSec is the measured bottleneck bandwidth (bits/s), rtt the measured
192	// path RTT, and avgWireFrameBytes the average on-wire outer-frame size (a full-MTU
193	// datagram plus frame.DataOverhead is the conservative choice). It fails fast on a
194	// non-positive input rather than emitting a nonsensical (zero/negative/Inf) sizing.
195	func SizePacingFromBDP(bandwidthBitsPerSec float64, rtt time.Duration, avgWireFrameBytes float64) (BDPSizing, error) {
196		if bandwidthBitsPerSec <= 0 {
197			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing bandwidth must be > 0 bit/s, got %g", bandwidthBitsPerSec)
198		}
199		if rtt <= 0 {
200			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing RTT must be > 0, got %s", rtt)
201		}
202		if avgWireFrameBytes <= 0 {
203			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing average wire frame size must be > 0 bytes, got %g", avgWireFrameBytes)
204		}
205		const bitsPerByte = 8.0
206		capacityFPS := bandwidthBitsPerSec / (bitsPerByte * avgWireFrameBytes)
207		burstFrames := capacityFPS * rtt.Seconds()
208		return BDPSizing{CapacityFPS: capacityFPS, BurstFrames: burstFrames}, nil
209	}

211	// bandwidthUnit is a recognised link_bandwidth suffix and its bit/s multiplier.
212	type bandwidthUnit struct {
213		suffix string
214		mult   float64
215	}
216	
217	// bandwidthUnits are the accepted operator-facing bandwidth suffixes, longest-first so
218	// "gbit" is matched before "bit". SI decimal multipliers (k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9) over a
219	// bit/s base; "bps" is accepted as an alias of "bit" (and "kbps"/"mbps"/"gbps" likewise).
220	var bandwidthUnits = []bandwidthUnit{
221		{"gbit", 1e9}, {"gbps", 1e9},
222		{"mbit", 1e6}, {"mbps", 1e6},
223		{"kbit", 1e3}, {"kbps", 1e3},
224		{"bit", 1}, {"bps", 1},
225	}
226	
227	// parseBandwidth parses an operator-declared link bandwidth such as "50Mbit", "1Gbit",
228	// or "500kbit" into bits per second. It requires an explicit bit/s unit suffix so a
229	// bare unitless number cannot be silently misread, and fails fast on an empty or
230	// otherwise unparseable value — a mistyped bandwidth is rejected at config load.
231	func parseBandwidth(s string) (float64, error) {
232		lower := strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(s))
233		if lower == "" {
234			return 0, errors.New("empty bandwidth")
235		}
236		for _, u := range bandwidthUnits {
237			if !strings.HasSuffix(lower, u.suffix) {
238				continue
239			}
240			num := strings.TrimSpace(lower[:len(lower)-len(u.suffix)])
241			val, err := strconv.ParseFloat(num, 64)
242			if err != nil {
243				return 0, fmt.Errorf("invalid number %q", num)
244			}
245			return val * u.mult, nil
246		}
247		return 0, fmt.Errorf("missing bit/s unit suffix (want e.g. %q)", "50Mbit")
248	}
249	
250	// FEC group-close deadline default (T24). Applied only when [fec] is enabled but
251	// leaves the deadline at its zero value, so a minimal `enabled = true` block emits
252	// parity for a partially-filled group promptly under low load rather than stranding
253	// it until the size threshold fills.
254	const defaultFECDeadline = 5 * time.Millisecond
255	
256	// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor mirrors adaptivefec.DefaultSafetyFactor (1.5), the
257	// simulation-proven controller default, restated here (with this cross-reference rather
258	// than an import) so a minimal `adaptive = true` block gets the proven tuning without
259	// coupling config to the controller package. NOTE: 1.5 sizes M for the mean loss with
260	// modest variance headroom; a tight residual SLA under a specific loss/geometry may need a
261	// higher factor (e.g. at 5% loss with K=10 the 1.5 default sizes M=1, ~1% residual — raise
262	// this to lift M and drive the residual down).
263	const defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor = 1.5
264	
265	// maxFECDeadline bounds the FEC group-close deadline at load time (T24, defect #4). It
266	// MUST stay at or below the multipath Bind's authoritative bound (bind.maxFECDeadline =
267	// resequencerTimeout/2 = 125ms): a group flushed by the deadline emits its parity
268	// `deadline` after opening, and the reconstructed frames must reach the receive
269	// resequencer BEFORE it skips the gap (its 250ms per-gap timeout) — otherwise recovery
270	// is structurally too late (the gap is skipped, the recovered frame dropped as past the
271	// release point) while /metrics would still count it reconstructed. Rejecting an
272	// over-large deadline at load makes that coupling fail-fast and explicit rather than
273	// silently defeating FEC. Kept in lockstep with bind.maxFECDeadline (the packages
274	// cannot import each other, so the value is mirrored with this cross-reference).
275	const maxFECDeadline = 125 * time.Millisecond
276	
277	// maxFECShards mirrors the Reed-Solomon field limit enforced in internal/fec: a
278	// coding group carries at most 256 shards (data + parity) total over GF(2^8). It
279	// is restated here so config load fails fast on an over-large ratio at the right
280	// locus, with the same bound the codec enforces internally.
281	const maxFECShards = 256
282	
283	// FEC configures the fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon forward-error-correction plane (T24).
284	// It is DISABLED by default (like pacing), so an existing config with no [fec] block
285	// runs the datapath exactly as before — FEC transparent, no parity on the wire. When
286	// enabled, each group of DataShards (K) inner datagrams is protected by ParityShards
287	// (M) parity frames, letting the receiver reconstruct up to M lost data frames per
288	// group at a fixed M/K parity overhead.
289	type FEC struct {
290		// Enabled turns the FEC plane on. When false every other field is ignored and the
291		// datapath carries no parity.
292		Enabled bool `toml:"enabled"`
293		// DataShards is K: the number of inner datagrams grouped before parity is emitted.
294		// Must be >= 1 when enabled.
295		DataShards int `toml:"data_shards"`
296		// ParityShards is M: the parity frames emitted per group and the maximum number of
297		// per-group data losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1 when enabled. In
298		// adaptive mode it is the CEILING (and the receiver's decoder cardinality) — the
299		// controller drives the per-group parity in [0,ParityShards] to track measured loss.
300		ParityShards int `toml:"parity_shards"`
301		// Deadline bounds grouping latency: a partially-filled group is flushed (parity
302		// emitted over its current data frames) once this much time has elapsed since its
303		// first frame. Defaults to defaultFECDeadline when enabled and left zero; must be
304		// > 0 after defaulting.
305		Deadline time.Duration `toml:"deadline"`
306		// Adaptive opts the send-side FEC into the closed-loop controller (T27/T29): the
307		// per-group parity count tracks the measured per-path loss instead of standing at
308		// the fixed ParityShards ratio, so a clean path spends near-zero overhead while a
309		// lossy one is masked. It is OFF by default, so an existing [fec] block keeps the
310		// fixed-ratio behaviour (T24) byte-for-byte. When true, ParityShards is reinterpreted
311		// as the controller's parity ceiling (see above); the receiver is unchanged because a
312		// group coded with fewer parity shards decodes against the ParityShards-ceiling codec
313		// unchanged (klauspost's parity is prefix-consistent).
314		Adaptive bool `toml:"adaptive"`
315		// SafetyFactor is the adaptive controller's headroom multiplier over the measured mean
316		// loss the per-group parity is sized to mask (adaptivefec.Config.SafetyFactor). It is
317		// the residual-loss LEVER: the controller sizes M so M/(K+M) >= SafetyFactor*loss, so a
318		// higher factor spends more parity to keep the post-recovery residual under a tighter
319		// bound against binomial per-group variance. Applies only in adaptive mode; defaults to
320		// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor (the simulation-proven controller default) when left zero
321		// AND target_residual is unset, and must be >= 1. Ignored (and must stay zero) in fixed
322		// mode, and mutually exclusive with target_residual (set one, not both).
323		SafetyFactor float64 `toml:"safety_factor"`
324		// TargetResidual is the adaptive controller's target POST-RECOVERY residual-loss SLA
325		// (a fraction in (0,1)) and the PRIMARY adaptive sizing surface (D26/T46): the
326		// controller derives the per-group parity M by inverting the binomial residual model
327		// E[max(0,D-M)]/K (D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss)) to the smallest M meeting this target,
328		// capped at the parity ceiling (parity_shards). It SUPERSEDES safety_factor — set
329		// EITHER a residual SLA (target_residual, recommended: it maps an operator's loss
330		// budget directly to redundancy) OR the bare headroom multiplier (safety_factor), never
331		// both. Applies only in adaptive mode; unset (0) selects the safety_factor path. Must
332		// be in (0,1) when set. Ignored (and must stay zero) in fixed mode.
333		TargetResidual float64 `toml:"target_residual"`
334	}
335	
336	// applyDefaults fills the group-close deadline when FEC is enabled and the deadline
337	// was left at zero. It is a no-op for a disabled block, so a config that never turns
338	// FEC on keeps an empty [fec] surface.
339	func (f *FEC) applyDefaults() {
340		if !f.Enabled {
341			return
342		}
343		if f.Deadline == 0 {
344			f.Deadline = defaultFECDeadline
345		}
346		// The SafetyFactor default is applied only in the legacy sizing mode: when
347		// target_residual is set it is the primary surface and safety_factor stays inert
348		// (0), so defaulting it would spuriously trip the mutual-exclusion check.
349		if f.Adaptive && f.TargetResidual == 0 && f.SafetyFactor == 0 {
350			f.SafetyFactor = defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor
351		}
352	}
353	
354	// validate enforces the FEC ratio invariants, failing fast so a mis-tuned parity
355	// ratio is rejected at load rather than panicking the codec at runtime. A disabled
356	// block needs no tuning. The bounds mirror internal/fec's own Config.validate.
357	func (f FEC) validate() error {
358		if !f.Enabled {
359			if f.Adaptive {
360				return errors.New("fec.adaptive = true requires fec.enabled = true (adaptive FEC is meaningless with the plane off)")
361			}
362			return nil
363		}
364		if f.DataShards < 1 {
365			return fmt.Errorf("fec.data_shards must be >= 1 when FEC is enabled, got %d", f.DataShards)
366		}
367		if f.ParityShards < 1 {
368			return fmt.Errorf("fec.parity_shards must be >= 1 when FEC is enabled, got %d", f.ParityShards)
369		}
370		if f.DataShards+f.ParityShards > maxFECShards {
371			return fmt.Errorf("fec.data_shards + fec.parity_shards must be <= %d (Reed-Solomon field limit), got %d", maxFECShards, f.DataShards+f.ParityShards)
372		}
373		if f.Deadline <= 0 {
374			return fmt.Errorf("fec.deadline must be > 0 when FEC is enabled, got %s", f.Deadline)
375		}
376		if f.Deadline > maxFECDeadline {
377			return fmt.Errorf("fec.deadline must be <= %s (safely below the receive resequencer's per-gap timeout so deadline-flushed recovery lands before the gap is skipped), got %s", maxFECDeadline, f.Deadline)
378		}
379		if f.Adaptive {
380			if f.TargetResidual != 0 {
381				// Residual-SLA sizing mode (primary): target_residual governs and safety_factor
382				// stays inert, so setting both is an ambiguous mis-config — reject it. NaN is
383				// caught explicitly because every ordered comparison against it is false.
384				if math.IsNaN(f.TargetResidual) || f.TargetResidual <= 0 || f.TargetResidual >= 1 {
385					return fmt.Errorf("fec.target_residual must be a finite value in (0,1), got %g", f.TargetResidual)
386				}
387				if f.SafetyFactor != 0 {
388					return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor and fec.target_residual are mutually exclusive; target_residual is the primary residual-SLA surface — set one, not both")
389				}
390			} else if f.SafetyFactor < 1 {
391				// SafetyFactor is defaulted to defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor when zero, so a value
392				// below 1 here is an explicit mis-set: the controller requires >= 1 (masking less
393				// than the mean loss is nonsensical), matching adaptivefec.Config.Validate.
394				return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor must be >= 1 in adaptive mode, got %g", f.SafetyFactor)
395			}
396		} else if f.SafetyFactor != 0 || f.TargetResidual != 0 {
397			return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor / fec.target_residual are only meaningful in adaptive mode (set fec.adaptive = true), got safety_factor=%g target_residual=%g", f.SafetyFactor, f.TargetResidual)
398		}
399		return nil
400	}

402	// Path is one physical WAN uplink. The edge binds each path's UDP socket to
403	// SourceAddr so the upstream router pins it to the intended WAN; the
404	// concentrator learns the real per-path endpoints from authenticated traffic.
405	type Path struct {
406		// Name is a stable human-readable identifier for the path (e.g. "starlink").
407		Name string `toml:"name"`
408		// SourceAddr is the local source IP the path's socket binds to.
409		SourceAddr netip.Addr `toml:"-"`
410		// SourceAddrRaw is the TOML string form of SourceAddr; parsed in normalize.
411		SourceAddrRaw string `toml:"source_addr"`
412		// DestAddr is the OPTIONAL per-path concentrator endpoint ("ip:port") the
413		// edge sends this path's datagrams to. It is unset on the concentrator (which
414		// learns edge endpoints from traffic) and optional on the edge: when a single
415		// public concentrator IP fronts all uplinks (the common source-routed case)
416		// the peer's wireguard endpoint is reused for every path; when the paths reach
417		// the concentrator on distinct addresses, set dest_addr per path.
418		DestAddr netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
419		// DestAddrRaw is the TOML string form of DestAddr; parsed in normalize.
420		DestAddrRaw string `toml:"dest_addr"`
421		// LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec is the OPERATOR-DECLARED bottleneck bandwidth of this
422		// uplink in bits/s, parsed from LinkBandwidthRaw in normalize. It is used ONLY to
423		// size the weighted scheduler's per-path pace from the bandwidth-delay product
424		// (SizePacingFromBDP) when the weighted policy runs with pacing ENABLED (T53, Q20);
425		// it is OPERATOR-DECLARED, not runtime-measured — wanbond never auto-tunes it live.
426		// Zero means "not declared" (the synthetic default pace is kept).
427		LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec float64 `toml:"-"`
428		// LinkBandwidthRaw is the TOML string form of the declared bandwidth, e.g.
429		// "50Mbit" / "1Gbit" / "500kbit" (SI bit/s units; the "bit" suffix may be written
430		// "bps"). Parsed in normalize; a non-positive or unparseable value fails fast.
431		LinkBandwidthRaw string `toml:"link_bandwidth"`
432		// LinkRTT is the OPERATOR-DECLARED baseline RTT of this uplink, parsed from
433		// LinkRTTRaw in normalize. It is the delay term of the bandwidth-delay-product pace
434		// burst (one RTT of in-flight frames); required (> 0) when LinkBandwidth is set and
435		// pacing is enabled under the weighted policy, ignored otherwise.
436		LinkRTT time.Duration `toml:"-"`
437		// LinkRTTRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of LinkRTT, e.g. "45ms". Parsed in
438		// normalize; an unparseable or non-positive value fails fast.
439		LinkRTTRaw string `toml:"link_rtt"`
440	}

443	type WireGuard struct {
444		PrivateKey Key    `toml:"private_key"`
445		Peers      []Peer `toml:"peers"`
446		// ListenPort is the UDP port the concentrator listens on; 0 on the edge.

450	// Peer is one WireGuard peer.
451	type Peer struct {
452		PublicKey Key `toml:"public_key"`
453		// Endpoint is the peer's SINGLE tunnel address (edge -> concentrator); the
454		// legacy single-endpoint config form, mutually exclusive with EndpointsRaw.
455		// Empty on the concentrator, which roams the edge's endpoint dynamically.
456		// A bare `endpoint = "..."` is normalized in resolveEndpoints to a one-
457		// element Endpoints list, so it stays behavior-identical to the pre-T54
458		// config surface.
459		Endpoint string `toml:"endpoint"`
460		// EndpointsRaw is the ORDERED list of concentrator (peer) endpoint address:port
461		// strings for the edge-side hub-failover config surface (Q18): index 0 is the
462		// active/primary concentrator, the rest are ordered standbys. Edge-only; a
463		// concentrator declaring it is a config error (validate). Mutually exclusive
464		// with the legacy Endpoint field.
465		EndpointsRaw []string `toml:"endpoints"`
466		// Endpoints is EndpointsRaw (or the single legacy Endpoint, as a one-element
467		// list) parsed to netip.AddrPort and de-duplicated; populated by
468		// resolveEndpoints in normalize(). This is the shape T57's hub-failover
469		// switch consumes: Endpoints[0] is the active concentrator, Endpoints[1:]
470		// are the ordered standbys to fail over to in order when all paths to the
471		// active one are down. A hostname entry (Q35) contributes NO element here at
472		// load time — resolution happens at runtime, not at config load (Q30) — so
473		// Endpoints only ever holds the literal-address entries, in order.
474		Endpoints []netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
475		// DNS is the explicit per-peer opt-in (Q29) for hostname endpoint entries: a
476		// hostname entry in endpoint/endpoints without dns = true is a config load
477		// error naming this flag. Default-off, so an existing IP-literal config never
478		// takes a new code path. Edge-only; a concentrator declaring dns = true is a
479		// config error, mirroring the endpoints-not-meaningful-for-concentrator rule.
480		DNS bool `toml:"dns"`
481		// EndpointSpecs is the ordered, typed per-entry parse of the endpoint/
482		// endpoints entries (Q35): each entry is either a literal address:port
483		// (IsName=false, Addr set — parsed with EXACTLY today's netip.ParseAddrPort
484		// path) or, when DNS opts in, a hostname:port (IsName=true, Host/Port set).
485		// Populated by resolveEndpoints in normalize(); order matches the TOML list.
486		EndpointSpecs []EndpointSpec `toml:"-"`
487		// AllowedIPs are the CIDR ranges routed to this peer.
488		AllowedIPs []string `toml:"allowed_ips"`
489		// PSK is this peer's OPTIONAL per-peer override of the outer-control PSK
490		// (G4 multi-peer concentrator groundwork). When unset, the top-level
491		// Config.PSK remains the single-peer default and this field has no effect —
492		// a legacy single-peer config carrying only the top-level `psk` parses and
493		// behaves byte-identically to before this field existed. No code path
494		// consumes PSK yet (no datapath change in T80); it is parsed and exposed
495		// only.
496		PSK Key `toml:"psk"`
497		// Name is this peer's OPTIONAL human-readable identifier (G4 multi-peer
498		// concentrator groundwork), analogous to Path.Name. Unset by default and
499		// not yet consumed by any code path.
500		Name string `toml:"name"`
501	}
502	
503	// EndpointSpec is one parsed peer endpoint entry (Q35). A literal address:port
504	// entry parses via netip.ParseAddrPort and sets Addr (IsName=false); a
505	// hostname:port entry (accepted only when the owning Peer opts in with
506	// dns = true) sets Host/Port and IsName=true instead — no resolution happens at
507	// config load (Q30 defers it to runtime).
508	type EndpointSpec struct {
509		// Host is the hostname, set only when IsName is true.
510		Host string
511		// Port is the port for a hostname entry, set only when IsName is true.
512		Port uint16
513		// Addr is the parsed literal address:port, set only when IsName is false.
514		Addr netip.AddrPort
515		// IsName reports whether this entry is a hostname (true) or an IP literal
516		// (false).
517		IsName bool
518	}
519	
520	// resolveEndpoints canonicalizes the peer's endpoint config into the ordered
521	// Endpoints list (Q18) and the ordered, typed EndpointSpecs list (Q35): the
522	// legacy single Endpoint field and the new ordered EndpointsRaw list are
523	// mutually exclusive input forms, but a bare `endpoint = "..."` is normalized
524	// here to a ONE-ELEMENT list — so it stays behavior-identical to the pre-T54
525	// single-defaultRemote config surface. Each entry is tried FIRST as a literal
526	// netip.AddrPort ("host:port"), the same format bind.Multipath.ParseEndpoint
527	// requires of the UAPI endpoint string at runtime — an all-literal config
528	// takes EXACTLY this path, unchanged, with the same errors and the same
529	// duplicate detection as before T67 (Q29). Only when that parse fails, AND the
530	// host portion is not itself a malformed IP literal, is the entry split as
531	// host:port and treated as a hostname, gated behind the peer's explicit DNS
532	// opt-in (Q29); a hostname entry without the opt-in is a config error naming
533	// the flag. No resolution happens here (Q30 defers it to runtime). Duplicates
534	// are rejected within each of the two namespaces: a literal duplicating a
535	// literal, or a hostname:port duplicating another hostname:port.
536	func (p *Peer) resolveEndpoints() error {
537		if p.Endpoint != "" && len(p.EndpointsRaw) > 0 {
538			return errors.New("endpoint and endpoints are mutually exclusive; endpoint is the single-entry legacy form of endpoints")
539		}
540		raw := p.EndpointsRaw
541		if p.Endpoint != "" {
542			raw = []string{p.Endpoint}
543		}
544		seen := make(map[netip.AddrPort]struct{}, len(raw))
545		seenNames := make(map[string]struct{}, len(raw))
546		endpoints := make([]netip.AddrPort, 0, len(raw))
547		specs := make([]EndpointSpec, 0, len(raw))
548		for _, s := range raw {
549			ap, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(s)
550			if err == nil {
551				if _, dup := seen[ap]; dup {
552					return fmt.Errorf("duplicate endpoint %q", s)
553				}
554				seen[ap] = struct{}{}
555				endpoints = append(endpoints, ap)
556				specs = append(specs, EndpointSpec{Addr: ap})
557				continue
558			}
559			host, portStr, splitErr := net.SplitHostPort(s)
560			if splitErr != nil {
561				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, err)
562			}
563			// The host portion parses as an IP: this is a malformed IP-literal entry
564			// (e.g. a bad port), not a hostname — report the ORIGINAL ParseAddrPort
565			// error rather than diverting it into the hostname path, so every
566			// IP-shaped entry keeps today's exact error.
567			if _, ipErr := netip.ParseAddr(host); ipErr == nil {
568				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, err)
569			}
570			if !p.DNS {
571				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: hostname endpoints require the peer's dns = true opt-in flag", s)
572			}
573			port, portErr := strconv.ParseUint(portStr, 10, 16)
574			if portErr != nil || port == 0 {
575				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: invalid port %q", s, portStr)
576			}
577			if hostErr := validateHostname(host); hostErr != nil {
578				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, hostErr)
579			}
580			nameKey := host + ":" + portStr
581			if _, dup := seenNames[nameKey]; dup {
582				return fmt.Errorf("duplicate endpoint %q", s)
583			}
584			seenNames[nameKey] = struct{}{}
585			specs = append(specs, EndpointSpec{Host: host, Port: uint16(port), IsName: true})
586		}
587		p.Endpoints = endpoints
588		p.EndpointSpecs = specs
589		return nil
590	}

592	// validateHostname reports whether host is a syntactically valid DNS hostname
593	// (RFC 1123 label rules): 1-253 characters total, each dot-separated label
594	// 1-63 characters of letters/digits/hyphens, no leading or trailing hyphen.
595	func validateHostname(host string) error {
596		if len(host) == 0 || len(host) > 253 {
597			return fmt.Errorf("hostname %q must be 1-253 characters", host)
598		}
599		for _, label := range strings.Split(host, ".") {
600			if len(label) == 0 || len(label) > 63 {
601				return fmt.Errorf("hostname label %q must be 1-63 characters", label)
602			}
603			if label[0] == '-' || label[len(label)-1] == '-' {
604				return fmt.Errorf("hostname label %q must not start or end with a hyphen", label)
605			}
606			for _, r := range label {
607				alnum := (r >= 'a' && r <= 'z') || (r >= 'A' && r <= 'Z') || (r >= '0' && r <= '9')
608				if !alnum && r != '-' {
609					return fmt.Errorf("hostname label %q contains invalid character %q", label, r)
610				}
611			}
612		}
613		return nil
614	}

616	// Amnezia holds the amneziawg-go obfuscation parameters. They must match on both
617	// ends for the handshake to succeed; they are defense-in-depth only.
618	//
619	// The block is "all-or-nothing": either every junk/size knob is left zero (plain
620	// WireGuard) or the whole obfuscation set (jc, jmin, jmax, s1, s2) is specified.
621	// A PARTIAL block silently produces an obfuscation profile the two ends cannot
622	// agree on, so validate rejects it at load (defect D1). The four magic headers
623	// (h1-h4) default to the standard message-type values 1..4 when omitted.
624	type Amnezia struct {
625		Jc   int    `toml:"jc"`
626		Jmin int    `toml:"jmin"`
627		Jmax int    `toml:"jmax"`
628		S1   int    `toml:"s1"`
629		S2   int    `toml:"s2"`
630		H1   uint32 `toml:"h1"`
631		H2   uint32 `toml:"h2"`
632		H3   uint32 `toml:"h3"`
633		H4   uint32 `toml:"h4"`
634	}
635	
636	// defaultMagicHeaders are the standard WireGuard message-type headers: initiation,
637	// response, cookie-reply, and transport. amneziawg-go treats any magic header <= 4
638	// as "use the standard type", so 1..4 is the canonical "headers not obfuscated"
639	// profile. wanbond emits them explicitly (rather than 0) so a configured amnezia
640	// block always carries a complete, self-consistent set of magic headers.
641	var defaultMagicHeaders = [4]uint32{1, 2, 3, 4}
642	
643	// Configured reports whether the amnezia block carries any obfuscation parameter.
644	// An all-zero block leaves the engine in plain WireGuard mode.
645	func (a Amnezia) Configured() bool {
646		return a.Jc != 0 || a.Jmin != 0 || a.Jmax != 0 || a.S1 != 0 || a.S2 != 0 ||
647			a.H1 != 0 || a.H2 != 0 || a.H3 != 0 || a.H4 != 0
648	}
649	
650	// applyDefaults fills in the standard magic headers (1..4) when the block is
651	// configured but no header was given, so the UAPI renderer emits an explicit,
652	// complete header set instead of h1=0..h4=0. It is a no-op for an unconfigured
653	// block and for one that already sets any header (a partial header set is left
654	// intact so validate can reject it).
655	func (a *Amnezia) applyDefaults() {
656		if !a.Configured() {
657			return
658		}
659		if a.H1 == 0 && a.H2 == 0 && a.H3 == 0 && a.H4 == 0 {
660			a.H1, a.H2, a.H3, a.H4 = defaultMagicHeaders[0], defaultMagicHeaders[1], defaultMagicHeaders[2], defaultMagicHeaders[3]
661		}
662	}

675	// Key is a 32-byte Curve25519 key or PSK, carried in TOML as standard base64.
676	type Key struct {
677		bytes [keyLen]byte
678		set   bool
679	}
680	
681	// UnmarshalText decodes a base64 key. An empty string leaves the Key unset so
682	// optional keys can be distinguished from present-but-invalid ones.
683	func (k *Key) UnmarshalText(text []byte) error {
684		if len(text) == 0 {
685			return nil
686		}
687		raw, err := base64.StdEncoding.DecodeString(string(text))
688		if err != nil {
689			return fmt.Errorf("key is not valid base64: %w", err)
690		}
691		if len(raw) != keyLen {
692			return fmt.Errorf("key must decode to %d bytes, got %d", keyLen, len(raw))
693		}
694		copy(k.bytes[:], raw)
695		k.set = true
696		return nil
697	}
698	
699	// IsSet reports whether the key was present and valid.
700	func (k Key) IsSet() bool { return k.set }
701	
702	// Bytes returns the raw key material.
703	func (k Key) Bytes() [keyLen]byte { return k.bytes }

705	// normalize parses the string-typed fields (addresses) into their typed forms.
706	func (c *Config) normalize() error {
707		for i := range c.Paths {
708			p := &c.Paths[i]
709			if p.SourceAddrRaw != "" {
710				addr, err := netip.ParseAddr(p.SourceAddrRaw)
711				if err != nil {
712					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid source_addr %q: %w", p.Name, p.SourceAddrRaw, err)
713				}
714				p.SourceAddr = addr
715			}
716			if p.DestAddrRaw != "" {
717				dst, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(p.DestAddrRaw)
718				if err != nil {
719					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid dest_addr %q: %w", p.Name, p.DestAddrRaw, err)
720				}
721				p.DestAddr = dst
722			}
723			if p.LinkBandwidthRaw != "" {
724				bw, err := parseBandwidth(p.LinkBandwidthRaw)
725				if err != nil {
726					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid link_bandwidth %q: %w", p.Name, p.LinkBandwidthRaw, err)
727				}
728				if bw <= 0 {
729					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: link_bandwidth must be > 0, got %q", p.Name, p.LinkBandwidthRaw)
730				}
731				p.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec = bw
732			}
733			if p.LinkRTTRaw != "" {
734				rtt, err := time.ParseDuration(p.LinkRTTRaw)
735				if err != nil {
736					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid link_rtt %q: %w", p.Name, p.LinkRTTRaw, err)
737				}
738				if rtt <= 0 {
739					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: link_rtt must be > 0, got %q", p.Name, p.LinkRTTRaw)
740				}
741				p.LinkRTT = rtt
742			}
743		}
744		for i := range c.WireGuard.Peers {
745			if err := c.WireGuard.Peers[i].resolveEndpoints(); err != nil {
746				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: %w", i, err)
747			}
748		}
749		c.Amnezia.applyDefaults()
750		// Derive the weighted pace from any operator-declared per-link bandwidth BEFORE
751		// applyDefaults, so it can (a) see the raw zero PerPathCapacityFPS to distinguish an
752		// explicit knob from the default and (b) set the derived capacity/burst that
753		// applyDefaults then leaves intact (it only fills a knob left at zero). Under a
754		// disabled or non-weighted pacing it is a no-op, so the synthetic default is kept.
755		if err := c.deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP(); err != nil {
756			return err
757		}
758		c.Scheduler.applyDefaults()
759		c.FEC.applyDefaults()
760		return nil
761	}
762	
763	// deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP sizes the weighted scheduler's per-path pace from the
764	// operator-declared per-link bandwidth (T53, Q20) via SizePacingFromBDP instead of the
765	// synthetic defaultPerPathCapacityFPS. It runs only under the weighted policy with
766	// pacing ENABLED and at least one declared link_bandwidth; otherwise a declared
767	// bandwidth is inert (pacing ships DISABLED by default), so an unrelated config is
768	// untouched. The value is OPERATOR-DECLARED and fixed at load — NOT runtime auto-tuning
769	// (Q20 rejected a live control loop for the pilot).
770	//
771	// The scheduler carries a SINGLE reference per-path capacity applied to every path's
772	// token bucket, so a heterogeneous link set is sized to the BOTTLENECK: the slowest
773	// declared link governs the shared pace, because pacing any path faster than the
774	// slowest link's capacity would let that link build the very standing queue pacing
775	// exists to prevent. All paths must therefore declare a bandwidth (all-or-nothing) so
776	// the bottleneck is well-defined across the whole path set.
777	func (c *Config) deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP() error {
778		s := &c.Scheduler
779		if s.Policy != PolicyWeighted || !s.PacingEnabled {
780			return nil
781		}
782		declared := 0
783		for i := range c.Paths {
784			if c.Paths[i].LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec > 0 {
785				declared++
786			}
787		}
788		if declared == 0 {
789			return nil // no declared bandwidth: the synthetic default pace is preserved.
790		}
791		if declared != len(c.Paths) {
792			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler pacing: link_bandwidth must be declared on ALL paths or none (got %d of %d) — the shared per-path pace is sized to the slowest declared link, which is undefined with a partial declaration", declared, len(c.Paths))
793		}
794		if s.PerPathCapacityFPS != 0 || s.PacingBurstFrames != 0 {
795			return errors.New("scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps / pacing_burst_frames and per-path link_bandwidth are mutually exclusive: declare the link bandwidth (BDP-derived pace) OR set the raw frame-slot knobs, not both")
796		}
797		var bottleneck BDPSizing
798		for i := range c.Paths {
799			p := &c.Paths[i]
800			if p.LinkRTT <= 0 {
801				return fmt.Errorf("path %q: link_rtt is required (> 0) when link_bandwidth is set under weighted pacing — it is the delay term of the bandwidth-delay-product burst", p.Name)
802			}
803			sz, err := SizePacingFromBDP(p.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec, p.LinkRTT, defaultAvgWireFrameBytes)
804			if err != nil {
805				return fmt.Errorf("path %q: %w", p.Name, err)
806			}
807			if i == 0 || sz.CapacityFPS < bottleneck.CapacityFPS {
808				bottleneck = sz
809			}
810		}
811		s.PerPathCapacityFPS = bottleneck.CapacityFPS
812		s.PacingBurstFrames = bottleneck.BurstFrames
813		return nil
814	}
815	
816	// applyDefaults selects active-backup when no policy is given and, only under the
817	// weighted policy, fills any weighted knob left at its zero value with its default.
818	// It is a no-op for the active-backup policy: those weighted knobs are inert there,
819	// so leaving them zero keeps the config surface for a P1 deployment empty.
820	func (s *SchedulerConfig) applyDefaults() {
821		if s.Policy == "" {
822			s.Policy = PolicyActiveBackup
823		}
824		if s.Policy != PolicyWeighted {
825			return
826		}
827		if s.PerPathCapacityFPS == 0 {
828			s.PerPathCapacityFPS = defaultPerPathCapacityFPS
829		}
830		if s.EngageFraction == 0 {
831			s.EngageFraction = defaultEngageFraction
832		}
833		if s.DisengageFraction == 0 {
834			s.DisengageFraction = defaultDisengageFraction
835		}
836		if s.CollapseDwell == 0 {
837			s.CollapseDwell = defaultCollapseDwell
838		}
839		if s.LoadTau == 0 {
840			s.LoadTau = defaultLoadTau
841		}
842		if s.PacingBurstFrames == 0 {
843			s.PacingBurstFrames = defaultPacingBurstFrames
844		}
845		if s.WeightRTTFloor == 0 {
846			s.WeightRTTFloor = defaultWeightRTTFloor
847		}
848		if s.WeightLossFloor == 0 {
849			s.WeightLossFloor = defaultWeightLossFloor
850		}
851	}
852	
853	// validate enforces the scheduler policy invariants. active-backup needs no tuning.
854	// The weighted policy fails fast on any out-of-range knob so a mis-tuned
855	// aggregation policy is rejected at load rather than misbehaving at runtime (the
856	// hysteresis band, in particular, is only a band when disengage < engage).
857	func (s SchedulerConfig) validate() error {
858		if !s.Policy.valid() {
859			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.policy must be %q or %q, got %q", PolicyActiveBackup, PolicyWeighted, s.Policy)
860		}
861		if s.Policy != PolicyWeighted {
862			return nil
863		}
864		if s.PerPathCapacityFPS <= 0 {
865			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps must be > 0 under the weighted policy, got %g", s.PerPathCapacityFPS)
866		}
867		if s.EngageFraction <= 0 || s.EngageFraction > 1 {
868			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.engage_fraction must be in (0,1], got %g", s.EngageFraction)
869		}
870		if s.DisengageFraction < 0 || s.DisengageFraction >= s.EngageFraction {
871			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.disengage_fraction must be in [0,engage_fraction=%g) to form a hysteresis band, got %g", s.EngageFraction, s.DisengageFraction)
872		}
873		if s.CollapseDwell < 0 {
874			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.collapse_dwell must be >= 0, got %s", s.CollapseDwell)
875		}
876		if s.LoadTau <= 0 {
877			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.load_tau must be > 0, got %s", s.LoadTau)
878		}
879		if s.PacingEnabled && s.PacingBurstFrames <= 0 {
880			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.pacing_burst_frames must be > 0 when pacing is enabled, got %g", s.PacingBurstFrames)
881		}
882		if s.WeightRTTFloor <= 0 {
883			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.weight_rtt_floor must be > 0 under the weighted policy, got %s", s.WeightRTTFloor)
884		}
885		if s.WeightLossFloor <= 0 {
886			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.weight_loss_floor must be > 0 under the weighted policy, got %g", s.WeightLossFloor)
887		}
888		return nil
889	}

891	// validate enforces the required-field invariants, failing on the first problem.
892	func (c *Config) validate() error {
893		if !c.Role.valid() {
894			return fmt.Errorf("role must be %q or %q, got %q", RoleEdge, RoleConcentrator, c.Role)
895		}
896		if len(c.Paths) == 0 {
897			return errors.New("at least one path is required")
898		}
899		seen := make(map[string]struct{}, len(c.Paths))
900		// seenSrc maps an already-claimed source_addr to the path that claimed it, so a
901		// second path reusing it can be rejected at LOAD naming both conflicting paths.
902		// The multipath bind Opens each path on (source_addr, listen_port), so two paths
903		// sharing a source_addr collide EADDRINUSE at the second ListenUDP (and on every
904		// re-Open after Down/Up, since the engine passes the fixed port back) — a
905		// misconfiguration that must fail fast at load, not at bring-up (defect D10).
906		seenSrc := make(map[netip.Addr]string, len(c.Paths))
907		for i, p := range c.Paths {
908			if p.Name == "" {
909				return fmt.Errorf("path %d: name is required", i)
910			}
911			if _, dup := seen[p.Name]; dup {
912				return fmt.Errorf("duplicate path name %q", p.Name)
913			}
914			seen[p.Name] = struct{}{}
915			if !p.SourceAddr.IsValid() {
916				return fmt.Errorf("path %q: source_addr is required", p.Name)
917			}
918			// Compare the UNMAPPED form: "192.0.2.10" (Is4) and "::ffff:192.0.2.10"
919			// (Is4In6) are distinct under netip == yet bind the identical v4 socket, so
920			// the two spellings must collide in the guard exactly as they do at bind.
921			src := p.SourceAddr.Unmap()
922			if prev, dup := seenSrc[src]; dup {
923				return fmt.Errorf("paths %q and %q share source_addr %s; each path must bind a distinct source address (a shared source collides EADDRINUSE at the second bind)", prev, p.Name, p.SourceAddr)
924			}
925			seenSrc[src] = p.Name
926		}
927		if !c.WireGuard.PrivateKey.IsSet() {
928			return errors.New("wireguard.private_key is required")
929		}
930		if len(c.WireGuard.Peers) == 0 {
931			return errors.New("at least one wireguard peer is required")
932		}
933		for i, peer := range c.WireGuard.Peers {
934			if !peer.PublicKey.IsSet() {
935				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: public_key is required", i)
936			}
937			// EndpointSpecs (not Endpoints) is the "any endpoint entry configured" check:
938			// a hostname-only peer contributes nothing to Endpoints at load time (Q30 —
939			// resolution is deferred to runtime) but must still count as configured.
940			if c.Role == RoleEdge && len(peer.EndpointSpecs) == 0 {
941				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: endpoint is required for the edge role", i)
942			}
943			// The concentrator learns the edge's endpoint dynamically (roaming across
944			// NAT rebinds); an ordered failover endpoint list is meaningless for it, so
945			// reject rather than silently ignore a mis-targeted config (Q18 is edge-side
946			// only).
947			if c.Role == RoleConcentrator && len(peer.EndpointSpecs) != 0 {
948				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: endpoint/endpoints is not meaningful for the concentrator role (it learns the edge's endpoint dynamically)", i)
949			}
950			// The DNS opt-in (Q29) is edge-only, mirroring the endpoints rule above: a
951			// concentrator learns the edge's endpoint dynamically, so a hostname
952			// endpoint config (and its dns = true opt-in) is meaningless there even
953			// when no endpoint/endpoints entries are present.
954			if c.Role == RoleConcentrator && peer.DNS {
955				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: dns is not meaningful for the concentrator role (it learns the edge's endpoint dynamically)", i)
956			}
957		}
958		if c.Role == RoleConcentrator && c.WireGuard.ListenPort == 0 {
959			return errors.New("wireguard.listen_port is required for the concentrator role")
960		}
961		if !c.PSK.IsSet() {
962			return errors.New("psk is required (authenticates outer control/probe frames)")
963		}
964		if err := c.Amnezia.validate(); err != nil {
965			return err
966		}

990		// both ends are forced to specify the same complete profile. A partial block

1001		// message type by its on-the-wire length, so the obfuscated initiation packet
1002		// (MessageInitiationSize + s1) and response packet (MessageResponseSize + s2)

1004		// rejected later by the engine's IpcSet ("new init size == new response size");

Author: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Date:   Mon Jul 13 23:46:58 2026 +0100

    config: add per-peer psk and name fields to config.Peer (T80)
    
    WireGuard.Peer gains optional PSK (toml: psk) and Name (toml: name) fields
    for the G4 multi-peer concentrator groundwork. Top-level Config.PSK stays
    the single-peer default: nothing normalizes/backfills a per-peer PSK from
    it, so a legacy single-peer config carrying only the top-level psk parses
    to a byte-identical Config (golden struct compare). No datapath change;
    the new fields are parsed and exposed only.
    
    Add TestLoadPeerPSKName (2-peer TOML, distinct per-peer psk+name each) and
    TestLoadSinglePeerLegacyPSKGoldenShape (legacy single-peer TOML, golden
    reflect.DeepEqual compare against a hand-built expected Config).
